questions.q: In which field of science did Asima Chatterjee primarily work?
questions.explanation: Asima Chatterjee was an organic chemist celebrated for her research on natural plant products.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee is best known for her research on which class of plant compounds?
questions.explanation: She is renowned for her pioneering work on alkaloids found in medicinal plants.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was the first Indian woman to earn which degree?
questions.explanation: She became the first Indian woman to be awarded a Doctor of Science (D.Sc.) degree, by an Indian university.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee developed drugs to treat which neurological condition?
questions.explanation: She contributed to the development of anti-epileptic drugs based on natural products.

questions.q: Besides anti-epileptic drugs, Asima Chatterjee's work also led to drugs against which disease?
questions.explanation: Her research contributed to the development of anti-malarial drugs.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was a scientist from which country?
questions.explanation: Asima Chatterjee was an Indian scientist, born in Kolkata (then Calcutta).

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee's research focused largely on compounds derived from what source?
questions.explanation: She studied chemical compounds, especially alkaloids, derived from medicinal plants.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was a chemist; what was her gender, making her achievements notable as firsts for women in Indian science?
questions.explanation: She was a woman whose pioneering achievements broke barriers for women in Indian science.

questions.q: Which Indian city, a major center of science, was Asima Chatterjee most closely associated with?
questions.explanation: She was born, educated, and worked primarily in Kolkata (Calcutta).

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee's work belongs to which broad area of chemistry dealing with carbon-based compounds from living things?
questions.explanation: Her field was natural products chemistry, a branch of organic chemistry studying compounds made by living organisms.

questions.q: From which university did Asima Chatterjee earn her doctoral degrees and spend much of her career?
questions.explanation: She earned her degrees from and built her career at the University of Calcutta.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee is especially noted for her chemistry of vinca alkaloids, derived from which plant?
questions.explanation: She studied vinca alkaloids derived from the periwinkle plant (genus Vinca).

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee also conducted notable research on which group of plant compounds important to traditional medicine?
questions.explanation: She carried out significant research on coumarins as well as alkaloids and terpenoids.

questions.q: In what decade was Asima Chatterjee born?
questions.explanation: Asima Chatterjee was born in 1917, placing her birth in the 1910s.

questions.q: Which prestigious Indian award did Asima Chatterjee receive in 1975 for her contributions?
questions.explanation: She was awarded the Padma Bhushan, one of India's highest civilian honours, in 1975.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was the first woman to receive a Doctor of Science (D.Sc.) from an Indian university; in roughly what year did she earn it?
questions.explanation: She received her D.Sc. from the University of Calcutta in 1944, the first Indian woman to do so.

questions.q: An anti-epileptic drug associated with Asima Chatterjee's research was marketed under what kind of name?
questions.explanation: Her anti-epileptic work is linked to the drug Ayush-56, developed from natural products.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was the first woman to be elected as General President of which major scientific body?
questions.explanation: In 1975 she became the first woman to be elected General President of the Indian Science Congress Association.

questions.q: Anti-malarial compounds Asima Chatterjee studied were related to which classic plant-derived antimalarial?
questions.explanation: Her anti-malarial research connected to plant alkaloids in the tradition of quinine, the classic antimalarial.

questions.q: Which scientific organization did Asima Chatterjee become a Fellow of, reflecting national recognition?
questions.explanation: She was an elected Fellow of the Indian National Science Academy and other Indian academies.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee's chemistry often combined modern organic chemistry with knowledge from which Indian medical tradition?
questions.explanation: She drew on Ayurvedic medicinal plants, applying modern chemistry to traditional remedies.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee founded and headed the department of chemistry at which Kolkata college early in her career?
questions.explanation: She founded and headed the chemistry department at Lady Brabourne College in Kolkata.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee carried out part of her early research abroad on which class of compounds with American chemist L. M. Parks and others?
questions.explanation: Her work abroad, including in the United States and Switzerland, centered on biologically active natural products.

questions.q: At which Swiss institution did Asima Chatterjee work with Nobel laureate Paul Karrer on natural products?
questions.explanation: She worked with Paul Karrer at the University of Zurich, studying biologically active natural compounds.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee held the prestigious Khaira Professorship of Chemistry at which institution?
questions.explanation: She was appointed the Khaira Professor of Chemistry at the University of Calcutta in 1962.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee was nominated by the President of India as a member of which house of the Indian Parliament?
questions.explanation: She was a nominated member of the Rajya Sabha, the upper house of India's Parliament, from 1982 to 1990.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee received the first Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar award given to a woman; in which subject was it?
questions.explanation: She received the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize in Chemical Sciences in 1961, the first woman to win it.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee's doctoral work in the 1930s-40s at Calcutta was guided by which noted Indian chemist?
questions.explanation: Her doctoral research at the University of Calcutta was advised by Prafulla Chandra Ray, a founder of modern Indian chemistry (she also worked with physicist Satyendra Nath Bose).

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee's research on indole alkaloids contributed to understanding compounds with what kind of pharmacological activity?
questions.explanation: Her work on indole and other alkaloids illuminated their central nervous system effects, underpinning anti-epileptic and related drugs.

questions.q: Asima Chatterjee died in 2006; for how many decades did she remain an active researcher and academic in chemistry?
questions.explanation: Her research career spanned roughly six decades, from the late 1930s into the early 2000s.
